name: Mayahuel
description: Mayahuel is the Aztec goddess of the maguey plant, and by extension, pulque, an alcoholic beverage derived from it. She is a fertility goddess associated with nourishment, abundance, and drunkenness. Often depicted with numerous breasts, symbolizing her role as a provider, Mayahuel is considered a vital figure in Aztec agricultural and social life. Her origins are rooted in the earth and its bounty, and she is sometimes considered an aspect of other mother goddesses. Legend states she was a beautiful mortal woman, hidden away by her grandmother, Tzitzimitl, to prevent her from marrying Quetzalcoatl. Quetzalcoatl, in the form of a tree branch, convinced her to flee with him. Tzitzimitl, upon discovering their escape, killed Mayahuel, leaving Quetzalcoatl heartbroken. From her remains sprung the first maguey plant, a testament to her sacrifice and enduring life-giving power. Pulque, born from the maguey, is regarded as a sacred drink, used in rituals and ceremonies, connecting humans to the divine. Her role extended beyond mere sustenance; she represented the intoxicating joy of life and the spiritual connection facilitated through ritual and communal celebration. Mayahuel's story underscores themes of sacrifice, transformation, and the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. She embodies the power of the feminine, the earth's generosity, and the transformative potential of both nourishment and intoxication.
subtitle: Goddess of Maguey and Pulque

keyMyths.title: Mayahuel's Escape with Quetzalcoatl
keyMyths.description: Mayahuel, hidden by her grandmother, escapes with Quetzalcoatl in the form of a tree branch. Tzitzimitl discovers their betrayal and murders Mayahuel.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Maguey Plant
keyMyths.description: From Mayahuel's remains, Quetzalcoatl buries her bones, and the first maguey plant sprouts, symbolizing her life-giving essence.

keyMyths.title: Pulque as a Sacred Drink
keyMyths.description: Pulque, derived from the maguey, becomes a sacred drink used in Aztec rituals and ceremonies, connecting humans with the divine realm.
